summary: McKenzie reviews CrazyEgg, a visual analytics tool that competes with Google's free offerings. While he praises CrazyEgg's exceptional usability and heatmap visualization, he notes that Google Analytics and Google Website Optimizer already provide the core functionality for free. He highlights the fundamental challenge of competing with a free product from Google, even when your user experience is significantly better. The post also explains why proper A/B split testing (randomized allocation) is superior to sequential testing, especially for businesses with cyclical traffic patterns.
key_points: CrazyEgg's onboarding is remarkably smooth — email, password, two-line form, one line of HTML, and you're running in 30 seconds — setting a usability standard Google should learn from
Competing with free Google products is an existential challenge: even superior UX struggles to justify a paid subscription when the free alternative already works

Proper A/B testing requires randomized splitting of concurrent visitors, not sequential batches, because cyclical business patterns (like teacher traffic dropping on weekends) will skew sequential results
Google Website Optimizer has the right methodology (true randomized splitting) but is so painful to use that McKenzie has procrastinated for months on implementing it
money_quotes: Put in email address, put in password, fill out two line form about page to test, copy one line of provided HTML into page, upload. 30 seconds or less to the shiny.

I have already climbed Mt. Analytics Learning Curve and can now bask in the awesome view — such as dicing visitors down by where they came from, watching search terms trends, and tracking click paths around my site. For free.
It comes down to the 'Do I really want to pay more than my web hosting budget for an analytics program?' question. Which would be 'Oh, heck yes!'… if I didn't already have Analytics.

I have been meaning to start using Website Optimizer for months but every time I do I get to the start of the process, figure its going to take me two hours to get anywhere, and then get distracted by other demands on my time.

If they could just bring their considerable usability chops to solving the biggest problem with Website Optimizer, which is that it is a freaking pain in the keister to use, I'd give them $9 a month and a gold star.
key_insight: A paid product can have vastly superior usability and still lose to a free competitor that's already entrenched — but there's a real opportunity in making powerful-but-painful free tools actually usable.
